Introduction
?

The circulation of a three-dimensional vector field along a closed, piecewise-smooth curve parametrized by for is given by

This is the same definition as the two-dimensional case. Also, like the two-dimensional case, measures the total work done by a force in moving a particle along a closed curve traversed once.

Remember that circulation is always measured with respect to our chosen orientation for (positive or negative).

Let's get some practice at computing circulation over three-dimensional curves.

A Review of Green's Theorem
?

To understand the following work, it's a good idea to review Green's theorem.

Suppose is a positively oriented, piecewise-smooth, and simple closed curve in and is the region enclosed by If we consider the two-dimensional vector field to have zero -component, i.e.,

and the scalar functions and have continuous partial derivatives in the interior of then Green's theorem can be written as follows:

Green's theorem relates the sum of the microscopic curls of over to the macroscopic curl of (i.e., the circulation) along the boundary of

Green's theorem is a special case of another important theorem, which we'll discuss shortly. But first, we need to look at the relationship between oriented surfaces and their bounding curves.

Induced Orientations of Boundary Curves
?

Suppose we have an oriented surface (not closed) whose boundary curve is the closed curve as shown below.

Whenever we select an orientation for our chosen orientation automatically induces a positive orientation for

For example, if we select the upward-pointing unit normal vector shown below, the positive orientation is counterclockwise when viewed from above.

Assume that you are standing on the curve with your head pointing upward (i.e., in the same direction as ). Then, if you traverse in the positive direction, the surface will always be on your left.

For example, if is upward-oriented, the projection of the bounding curve onto the -plane runs counterclockwise when viewed from above.

Now, suppose we select the opposite orientation for shown below.

By choosing the opposite orientation for the positive orientation for is clockwise when viewed from above!

If you're not convinced, grab a child's doll, a toothbrush, or any other object with some kind of head, and mark its left and right sides (be careful not to damage it!). Then, walk it around the edge of a table so that its left side is always next to the table surface. If you turn it upside down, you will see that the object must traverse the table's edge in the opposite direction for the table surface to stay on the left.

Stokes' Theorem
?

We're now ready to state the main result of the lesson:

Let be an oriented piecewise-smooth surface bounded by a simple, closed, piecewise-smooth boundary with positive orientation. If a vector field has continuous partial derivatives for all its components in an open region in space that contains then

This result is known as Stokes' theorem and can be considered a generalization of Green's theorem. Let's spend some time building some intuition behind this result.

Suppose that is a vector field in Stokes' theorem states that the sum of the microscopic curls of on the surface equals the macroscopic curl of at the boundary of

Consider the left-hand side of the above picture, and suppose we have a point that lies on

The magnitude of the flux of through at is given by

We can think of this quantity as the amount of "swirl" of at that acts tangentially to at

If we sum (integrate) the flux of the curl through at every point over the entire surface we get

which we usually write as

This expression gives us the left-hand side of Stokes' theorem.

Let's now look at the right-hand side of our image. The macroscopic curl is simply the circulation of along the boundary of

Equating the sum of the microscopic curls with the circulation, we have

Stokes' theorem can be summarized in a single picture as follows:

We can immediately deduce a remarkable fact. Notice the value of the surface integral depends only on the circulation at the boundary. Therefore, within a given vector field, if two surfaces and have the same boundary then their respective surface integrals will be equal!

Using Stokes' Theorem to Evaluate Line Integrals
?

Calculating the circulation of a vector field along a closed, piecewise smooth curve can be time-consuming because it often requires splitting into several parts, evaluating a line integral along each one, and calculating their sum.

However, in such cases, we can often use Stokes' theorem to reduce the amount of work required by converting this sum of line integrals to a single surface integral.

For example, suppose that is the intersection of the paraboloid and the boundary of the infinite vertical column enclosed by the planes and Suppose also that is oriented counter-clockwise when viewed from above, and

Let's use Stokes' theorem to find the circulation of along .

We first compute the partial derivatives of

By computing the curl of we obtain

Next, we substitute the equation into expression for

So, we have

where is the region enclosed by the projection of onto the -plane, shown below.

Evaluating this integral using the usual methods, we get

Therefore, we conclude that

Note: Stokes theorem tells us that depends only on the circulation of at the boundary of Therefore, we could have evaluated our line integral by selecting any surface with as its bounding curve and integrating over this surface.

There are many surfaces whose boundary is the curve representing the intersection of the paraboloid and the plane One such surface would be the plane with the equation

We first compute the partial derivatives of the graph

Next, we substitute the equation into the expression for

where is region enclosed by the projection of onto the -plane.

Now, note that the projection of onto the -plane is the circle of radius centered at the origin. Therefore, using the change of variables to polar coordinates
